answers: Wrote /Users/kompzen/Offline/LLM-Graphrag/GraphRAG-chunksize/drift_trace_with_answer_20260223_180923.json FINAL ANSWER: Summary - short - Most likely root cause: a mismatch between the sensitivity label's encryption ACL (the label is granting decryption to a different AAD group) and the SharePoint folder ACL (which is granting access via a different group/object). Token / SSO caching makes this appear intermittent (older cached tokens sometimes include the "old" group claim and work; refreshed tokens lack the required label group and are denied). This exact failure pattern is the primary finding in the incident records you supplied [Data: Sources (30); Reports (8)]. Why this explains "same link, same user, different outcome" - Sensitivity labels that enforce encryption require the user's token to carry the group/claim the label allows (decrypt rights). If the label references Compliance_Core but SharePoint ACLs reference SP_Compliance_Main / COMPLIANCE_TEAM (different AAD objects), a user can be shown as permitted by SharePoint yet be unable to decrypt the files controlled by the label. Token caching (old vs new token claims) produces the incognito vs normal‑session difference you observed [Data: Sources (30); Sources (31)]. - A related, common root is an Azure AD Connect / same‑displayName collision (cloud group vs on‑prem group with different objectIDs) which can silently replace the cloud group that included guests - that also fits the external‑guest/ACL divergence pattern recorded in your tenant incidents [Data: Sources (13); Reports (64)]. Immediate (short‑term) actions (can be done by Helpdesk / 1st‑level) 1. Force a full sign‑out and token refresh for affected users: sign out of all Office desktop apps and the browser (or ask users to test in Incognito). If that restores access, it strongly indicates a stale‑token/claim issue [Data: Sources (38); Sources (30)]. 2. If you need a quick workaround, add an affected user temporarily to the AAD group the label grants (Compliance_Core) - investigators saw that adding a user to the label group immediately restored access for that user [Data: Sources (30)]. 3. Collect and attach these artifacts before escalating: one affected username, exact timestamps of a failing and a succeeding attempt, a screenshot of the SharePoint Manage Access view for the folder, and a screenshot/export of the Purview label encryption panel (which group the label allows). These are the minimal items 2nd‑level will need [Data: Sources (30); Reports (31)]. Root fix (requires 2nd‑level / Identity + Compliance + SharePoint) - Align the objects used by label and ACL: - Option A (preferred for minimal disruption): reconfigure the sensitivity label (or the label policy) so the label's allowed/decrypt group is the exact AAD group object that the SharePoint ACL uses (match by ObjectID, not display name). Or reconfigure the SharePoint ACL to use the group object the label expects. Do not rely on display names - compare GUIDs [Data: Sources (30); Sources (13)]. - Option B: if the label was applied after unique permissions and "Preserve custom permissions" was not enabled, reapply the label with Preserve custom permissions (or reapply after aligning groups) so encryption maps to the folder's existing ACL mapping [Data: Sources (31); Reports (63)]. - If you find duplicate groups (same displayName, different objectId) due to Azure AD Connect, consolidate the groups and run a full sync so the authoritative object contains intended members (including guests) - this is the documented remediation used in similar incidents [Data: Sources (13); Reports (64)]. - After making identity/group/label changes, force token revocation (or advise users to sign out) so clients receive fresh tokens with the corrected group claims [Data: Sources (31); Reports (8)]. Validation steps (what 2nd‑level should do) - Reproduce with a disposable test user: add to the label group and verify access, then remove and verify denial - the incident team used this to prove label enforcement [Data: Sources (30)]. - Pull Azure AD sign‑in logs for sample users and compare token claims before/after refresh to show COMPLIANCE_TEAM vs Compliance_Core claim presence; correlate sign‑in timestamps with the folder access attempts [Data: Sources (31); Sources (38)]. - Export SharePoint permission principals (drive item permissions via Graph) and Graph / Azure AD queries of groups named COMPLIANCE_TEAM / Compliance_Core to compare objectIds and onPremisesSyncEnabled flags (detect cloud vs synced objects) [Data: Sources (13); Reports (8)]. Who should own the ticket? - Start as a 1st‑level ticket for triage: Helpdesk should run the sign‑out/incognito tests, collect screenshots, and try the temporary add‑to‑label‑group test if permitted. If evidence points to a label ↔ ACL mismatch, duplicate group objectIDs, or changes to label configuration are required, escalate to 2nd‑level (Identity / BU Infrastructure - M365 / Purview/Compliance and SharePoint engineering). The incidents in your records followed exactly this escalation path [Data: Sources (30); Sources (13)]. What to hand to 2nd‑level when you escalate (minimum evidence) - Folder path and exact timestamp of when label was applied (Purview audit), SharePoint Manage Access screenshot, Purview label encryption screenshot (which AAD group is allowed), Azure AD sign‑in entries (failing and succeeding) for at least one user (raw JSON or exported entries), and a sample user who reproduced the incognito vs normal behaviour. That evidence lets identity engineers check token claims, objectIDs, and Azure AD Connect history quickly [Data: Sources (30); Reports (8, 13)]. If you want, I can also: - Provide exact Microsoft Graph / PowerShell commands to export the folder's permissions, list groups named COMPLIANCE_TEAM/Compliance_Core and their objectIds, and query sign‑in logs for group‑claim differences; or - Draft a short runbook for Helpdesk to collect the artifacts safely (screenshots, sign‑in timestamps) before escalation. References (incident evidence summarized) - The label vs ACL mismatch with token caching as the cause is documented in your incident records [Data: Sources (30); Reports (8)]. - Token refresh / forced sign‑out as an effective first‑line remediation is recorded in helpdesk testing notes [Data: Sources (38); Sources (30)]. - The cloud vs on‑prem group collision and remediation (consolidate groups + full Azure AD Connect sync) is documented in the related ticket work [Data: Sources (13); Reports (64)]. If you want those PowerShell/Graph snippets or a one‑page runbook for Helpdesk to execute now, tell me whether you have Global Admin / Compliance Admin rights available (or whether you want only the read‑only queries) and I'll produce copy‑paste commands.
